title: Mental Models — Designing for What Users Already Know
content: A mental model is the internal representation a user has of how a system works. Users bring mental models from prior experience — they expect new systems to behave like ones they already know. Design insight: the closer your UI matches users' existing mental models, the lower the learning curve. Violations of mental models cause confusion and distrust, even if the new design is 'objectively better'. Famous mental model failure: early touchscreen keyboards — users expected QWERTY because it was their existing model, not because it was optimal. Applied: study what tools your users come from, model your interaction patterns on their existing knowledge. Don't make users rebuild their mental model unless you're delivering significantly more value.

title: Cognitive Load — The Brain's Processing Budget
content: Cognitive load is the total amount of mental effort being used at any moment. Three types: Intrinsic (the inherent complexity of the task itself), Extraneous (complexity added by poor design — always eliminate this), Germane (mental effort that creates learning and understanding — sometimes valuable). UI design goal: minimise extraneous load so the user's mental budget is spent on the task, not figuring out the interface. Practical reductions: break complex tasks into steps, use progressive disclosure, reduce choices, use recognition over recall, maintain consistency. The paradox of choice (Barry Schwartz): more options often create more anxiety and worse decisions. 3–5 choices is often the usable ceiling before decision fatigue sets in.

title: Hick's Law — Fewer Choices, Faster Decisions
content: Hick's Law: the time to make a decision increases logarithmically with the number of options. Formula: T = b × log2(n + 1). Practical implication: doubling choices doesn't double decision time — it increases it by roughly 1 step. But the effect compounds. Applied to UI: limit navigation items to 5–7, reduce form fields to minimum viable, use defaults for non-critical choices, break wizard steps into clear single-choice moments. The counter-application: for expert users who know what they want, more choices visible = faster task completion. Hick's Law argues for simplicity in onboarding and clarity in navigation — not necessarily minimal overall functionality.

title: Fitts's Law — Size and Distance Define Click Effort
content: Fitts's Law: the time to acquire a target is a function of its size and distance. Larger targets are faster to click. Closer targets are faster to reach. The edges and corners of screens are effectively 'infinite' targets — the cursor stops there. Applied to UI: primary actions should be large and positioned where the cursor already is. Destructive actions should be small and far from the natural flow. The Mac Dock and menu bar exploit screen edges. Mobile: 44×44pt minimum touch target (Apple HIG), 48×48dp (Material Design). Common failure: tiny close buttons far from the content they close, or confirm/cancel buttons both the same size when one is far more critical.

title: Jakob's Law — Users Spend Most Time on Other Sites
content: Jakob's Law: users spend most of their time on other websites/apps. They expect your site to work the same way. This is the strongest argument against novel interaction patterns. Innovation in UX has a cost — every new pattern requires user learning investment. The benefit must exceed the learning cost. Applied: don't invent new navigation patterns unless you have overwhelming evidence they solve a real problem your users have. Use established patterns for: shopping carts (top right), search (top centre or top right), hamburger (mobile top left or right), settings (user avatar or gear icon). The only time novelty earns its cost is when it solves a problem users can't currently solve at all.

title: Miller's Law — Working Memory Has a Limit
content: Miller's Law: working memory can hold 7 (±2) items at once — often cited as 'the magical number seven'. Modern cognitive science refines this to 4 chunks for most adults. UI implication: don't present more than 4–7 independent items for users to hold in mind simultaneously. Chunking helps: group related items so they count as one chunk not many. Navigation groupings, form sections, and onboarding steps should all be chunked. Applied: a 12-item navigation becomes manageable if grouped into 3–4 clusters. A 20-step onboarding becomes bearable if chunked into 5 phases of 4. Miller's Law also explains why phone numbers are formatted in groups: 555-867-5309 is 3 chunks, not 10 digits.

title: Anchoring Bias — The First Number Wins
content: Anchoring: people rely heavily on the first piece of information they encounter (the 'anchor') when making decisions. In pricing UI: show the higher price first or display a 'was' price to anchor perceived value. In onboarding: the first step's framing anchors how users perceive the entire experience. In comparison tables: the first column anchors the evaluation criteria. Design applications: present your recommended/premium tier in the middle column (anchored against a more expensive option on its right). Show total value before revealing the price. In error messages: lead with what the user can do, not what went wrong — anchors the experience in agency not failure.

title: Peak-End Rule — Memory is Not an Average
content: Kahneman's Peak-End Rule: people remember experiences primarily by their peak (most intense moment) and their ending, not the average. A mostly poor experience with a great ending is remembered more positively than a consistently mediocre one. Applied to UX: the last interaction in a flow disproportionately defines the entire experience. Make success screens, completion states, and empty states excellent — they're the 'ending'. Find your experience's emotional peak and design it intentionally (an onboarding celebration, a first successful action). The most overlooked implication: a slow, frustrating checkout that ends with a perfect order confirmation creates a better memory than a smooth checkout with a boring confirmation.

title: Endowment Effect — Ownership Increases Perceived Value
content: The endowment effect: people value things more once they feel ownership of them. Applied to onboarding design: letting users customise, name, or configure something before asking for payment creates a sense of ownership that increases conversion. 'Your workspace is ready' is more powerful than 'Create a workspace'. Applied to data: showing users their data (even after a free trial) makes them less likely to churn — they've built something. Applied to shopping: letting users add to cart and configure before revealing full price. The endowment effect explains why free trials work: users build attachment to the product, and cancellation feels like a loss (loss aversion) more than forgoing a gain.

title: Variable Reward Schedules — The Slot Machine Pattern
content: Variable reward schedules (Skinner's operant conditioning) produce the highest engagement and most compulsive behaviour. Applied by: social media feeds (unpredictable good content), pull-to-refresh (will there be new content?), notification badges, likes and reactions. These patterns drive engagement but must be used ethically. In product design: use variable rewards to encourage healthy habits (fitness streaks, learning apps), not to exploit compulsive checking. The ethical line: is the user getting genuine value from the variable reward, or are they just being kept engaged for engagement metrics? Design for: user goals, not DAU metrics. Variable reward UX has produced significant societal harm (social media anxiety, compulsive phone use) and is under increasing regulatory scrutiny.

title: Dark Patterns — Design That Works Against Users
content: Dark patterns are UI designs that deliberately deceive or manipulate users against their interests. Harry Brignull's taxonomy: Trick questions (confusing opt-out language), Sneak into basket (adding items without consent), Roach motel (easy to get in, hard to get out — subscription cancellation flows), Privacy zuckering (confusing privacy settings that default to data sharing), Misdirection (drawing attention away from the thing you should see), Confirmshaming ('No thanks, I don't want to save money'), Hidden costs (revealing fees at final checkout step), Bait and switch (advertised feature doesn't deliver), Disguised ads (ads styled as content). Many dark patterns are now illegal under EU/UK consumer protection law. Beyond legality: they destroy trust and increase churn long-term.

title: Ethical Nudges — Designing for Better Decisions
content: Nudge theory (Thaler & Sunstein): the way choices are presented significantly influences which option is selected, without restricting freedom. Ethical nudge design: set good defaults (opt-in vs opt-out can swing adoption by 30–70%), use social proof ('Most popular' label on a pricing tier), reduce friction for desired actions, increase friction for harmful ones. Default power: if privacy settings default to 'share with everyone', most users will never change them. Defaults are design decisions with enormous social impact. Ethical checklist: Does this nudge align user behaviour with user goals? Does it increase or decrease user autonomy? Would users feel manipulated if they understood the design? Nudge design and dark patterns are two ends of the same spectrum — intention and transparency separate them.

title: Emotional Design — Norman's Three Levels
content: Don Norman's three levels of emotional design: Visceral (immediate, pre-conscious response to appearance — 'it looks beautiful'), Behavioural (pleasure and effectiveness of use — 'it works well'), Reflective (conscious evaluation, meaning, and personal identity — 'it says something about who I am'). Great products operate on all three levels. Visceral design influences first impressions within 50ms. Behavioural design determines long-term satisfaction and retention. Reflective design drives brand loyalty and advocacy — 'I use Apple because I'm creative'. Applied: don't treat aesthetics as decoration (visceral) separate from function (behavioural). They are inseparable in user experience. The most common mistake: excellent functional design with no visceral or reflective layer — products that 'work but nobody loves'.
